---
title: "Continuous Random Variables: The Probability Density Function"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Probability Foundations"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

POINT  = "#2b3a67"     # data / samples
LINE   = "#c1121f"     # the density curve / theoretical value
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # highlights / areas
GREY   = "#6c757d"
FILL   = "#9fb3d1"     # shaded area under a density
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the continuous half of the fork that opens at
> [PROB-02 *Random Variables: Turning Outcomes into Numbers*], which built the one object
> that describes *any* random number — the **cumulative distribution function**
> $F(x)=P(X\le x)$ — and previewed that distributions split into the **discrete** (jumps in
> $F$) and the **continuous** (a smoothly rising $F$). Its sibling [PROB-03 *Discrete Random
> Variables: The Probability Mass Function*] handled the jumps with a probability *mass*
> function. This article handles the smooth case: where every single value has probability
> zero, yet probability is spread across a continuum like a *density* of mass along a wire.
> We build the **probability density function** $f(x)=F'(x)$ from the Calculus-I
> fundamental theorem, show why a density is not a probability, and derive the
> change-of-variables rule for a monotone transform. It assumes [PROB-02] and floor-level
> integration; it sets up [PROB-05 *Expectation*] (the integral $\int x f(x)\,dx$), the
> continuous distribution catalog [PROB-13 *Normal*] and [PROB-14 *Gamma, Exponential &
> Beta*], and the vector change-of-variables of [PROB-18].

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

For a random variable that can land *anywhere* in an interval — a waiting time, a voltage,
a measurement error — the question "what is the probability it equals exactly $3.0$?" has a
startling answer: **zero.** Not "very small," exactly zero, and for every single value at
once. This article explains why that is forced rather than paradoxical, and how we recover
the ability to compute probabilities anyway. The instrument is the **probability density
function** $f(x)$, defined as the derivative of the cumulative distribution function
$F(x)=P(X\le x)$. The Calculus-I fundamental theorem of calculus then turns probabilities
into areas: $P(a\le X\le b)=\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$. The single claim this article defends is
that **a density is not a probability but a *rate* — probability per unit length** — which
is why it may legally exceed $1$, why only its integral over a set carries meaning, and why
transforming the variable bends the density by the derivative of the transform. We follow
this from the definition through three honest worked examples (uniform, triangular,
exponential) to the change-of-variables formula and the edges where the whole continuous
picture frays.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
For a continuous random variable, **probability is an area, not a height.** Each individual
value has probability $0$; what carries the probability is a **density** $f(x)=F'(x)$ — the
*rate* at which probability accumulates as you sweep $x$ to the right. To get the
probability that $X$ lands in an interval, you **add up the density across it**:
$$P(a\le X\le b)=\int_a^b f(x)\,dx.$$
Because $f$ is a rate (probability per unit length) and not a probability, it may rise
above $1$; the only constraints are that it never goes negative and that the total area
under it is $1$.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The equation $f=F'$ will look like a definition pulled
from a hat, but by the end you should see it as the only sensible thing it could be: $F(x)$
is *accumulated* probability up to $x$, and the density is how fast that accumulation is
happening right at $x$ — exactly the relationship between a running total and its rate that
the fundamental theorem of calculus is about. Hold onto "density is a rate"; it resolves
every apparent paradox in this article, including the headline one — $P(X=x)=0$ everywhere,
and yet $X$ certainly lands *somewhere*.

# Why we are here: the problem a continuum forces {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **when a quantity can take any value in a
continuum, how do we put probabilities on it without contradiction?** Every move below is
an answer to a piece of that question. If a step ever feels arbitrary, come back here.

## The crack that the discrete picture cannot paper over

The previous article, [PROB-03], handled a random variable that takes *countably many*
values — a die's face, a count of defects, the number of heads in ten flips. There, life is
easy: you assign each value $x$ a chunk of probability $p(x)=P(X=x)$, you insist those
chunks are non-negative and sum to $1$, and the probability of any event is the sum of the
chunks it contains. The probability *lives on the points*.

Now change the question by one word. Instead of "how many heads in ten flips," ask "**how
long** until the next bus arrives?" The answer can be $4$ minutes, or $4.1$, or
$4.137\ldots$ — any non-negative real number. Or "what is the **exact** measurement error
of this voltmeter?" — any real number, positive or negative. The set of possible values is
no longer a list you can write down; it is an interval, an *uncountable* continuum.

Try to carry the discrete machinery across and it breaks immediately. Suppose we wanted to
assign a positive probability $p(x)=P(X=x)>0$ to each value $x$ in the interval $[0,1]$.
There are uncountably many such values. Here is the contradiction, and it is worth seeing in
full because it is *the* reason densities exist.

Pick any positive number $\varepsilon>0$, however tiny. Ask: how many values $x$ can have
$P(X=x)\ge\varepsilon$? If there were $N$ such values, the total probability would be at
least $N\varepsilon$, and since total probability cannot exceed $1$ (that is Kolmogorov's
axiom $P(\Omega)=1$, built in [PROB-00]), we must have $N\varepsilon\le 1$, so
$N\le 1/\varepsilon$. That is a *finite* number. So **for each fixed $\varepsilon$, only
finitely many values can have probability at least $\varepsilon$.**

Now take the values with positive probability and sort them by how much they carry: those
with $P\ge \tfrac12$ (finitely many), those with $\tfrac14\le P<\tfrac12$ (finitely many),
those with $\tfrac18\le P<\tfrac14$ (finitely many), and so on down the powers of two.
*Every* value with positive probability falls into exactly one of these bins, because its
probability, being positive, eventually exceeds some $\tfrac{1}{2^k}$. A countable union of
finite sets is countable. So **at most countably many values can carry positive
probability** — no matter what distribution we cook up.

But our continuum $[0,1]$ is *uncountable*. So at most a countable handful of its points
could carry any probability, and the uncountably many others must each carry exactly $0$.
If we want the probability to be spread *evenly* over the whole interval — no point special
— there is no escape: **every individual value must have probability zero.**

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"If $P(X=x)=0$ for every value, isn't the total probability $0$, contradicting
$P(\Omega)=1$?"* This is the sharpest objection and you should have the answer ready. No:
you cannot recover the total by "adding up" the point probabilities, because there are
**uncountably many** of them, and Kolmogorov's additivity axiom only promises that
probability adds over **countable** collections of disjoint events (see [PROB-00]). An
uncountable sum of zeros is simply not a defined operation in the axioms — it is not "zero,"
it is *undefined*. The total probability is recovered a different way, by **integration**,
which is the limit of sums over finer and finer *partitions* (countably many pieces at each
stage), not a sum over the individual points. That is precisely the gap the density fills.
:::

So the continuum hands us a genuine problem: the object that worked for discrete variables —
probability sitting on points — is mathematically dead on arrival. We need to relocate the
probability from the points to the *intervals*.

## The physical analogy that names the fix

The resolution is older than probability and comes from physics. Ask: "what is the mass of
a steel wire *at the single point* $3$ cm from its left end?" Zero — a point has no length,
hence no mass. Yet the wire certainly has mass, distributed along it. The way we describe
that mass is not by assigning mass to points but by a **linear mass density** $\rho(x)$,
measured in grams per centimeter. The mass of any *segment* is the density integrated over
it: $\int_a^b \rho(x)\,dx$ grams. The density at a point can be large (a dense alloy) and
even exceed $1$ g/cm, yet the mass *at* the point is still zero, because mass is
density $\times$ length and the length of a single point is zero.

Probability for a continuous variable is exactly this, with "probability" in place of
"mass" and "per unit of the variable" in place of "per centimeter." The probability of a
single value is zero (it has no length to spread over); the probability of an interval is a
density integrated across it. **The probability density function is the probability analogue
of mass density.** That one analogy carries the entire article, and it is the *why* we will
keep returning to: every property of $f$ has a direct counterpart in a mass density, and we
prove each one below rather than lean on the picture. Non-negativity (you cannot have
negative mass in a segment, just as a non-decreasing $F$ cannot have negative slope —
@thm-valid-density), total area one (the whole wire has its full mass, just as $X$ lands
somewhere with probability $1$ — @thm-valid-density), the freedom to exceed $1$ (a dense
alloy has more than $1$ g/cm, just as a sharply-peaked density tops $1$ — the pitfall in
@sec-density-laws), and the way it transforms (re-coordinatizing the wire rescales the
density by the local stretch, just as $Y=g(X)$ rescales $f$ by the Jacobian —
@thm-change-of-vars). The analogy supplies the *intuition*; the theorems below supply the
*proof*.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why $P(X=x)=0$ in one sentence?"* "A single value has no width, and for a continuous
variable probability is spread out as a density per unit width, so a zero-width slice
catches zero probability — just like a single point of a wire has zero mass." That sentence,
delivered cleanly, is what an interviewer wants. The contradiction argument above is the
*proof*; the wire is the *picture*.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before any formula, picture the two functions we will relate and watch the discrete picture
*melt* into the continuous one. @fig-melt does this melt explicitly: it takes a discrete
variable, splits its mass into ever-finer pieces, and watches the staircase CDF smooth into
a curve and the mass spikes flatten into a density.

```{python}
#| label: fig-melt
#| fig-cap: "From mass to density. We model a wait time as a discrete variable on a grid, then refine the grid (top row, left to right: 6, 16, then 60 buckets), keeping the *total* probability fixed at 1. Top: the probability of each bucket shrinks toward 0 as buckets multiply — the discrete pmf 'spikes' flatten. Bottom: but the **height per unit width** (bucket probability divided by bucket width) converges to a stable curve — the density $f$. The density is what survives the refinement; the individual probabilities do not."
from scipy import stats

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(9.0, 5.2), sharex='col')

# An underlying continuous shape (a gamma-like bump) we will discretize.
true = stats.gamma(a=3.0, scale=1.4)
xgrid = np.linspace(0, 14, 600)
dens = true.pdf(xgrid)

for col, K in enumerate((6, 16, 60)):
    edges = np.linspace(0, 14, K + 1)
    centers = 0.5 * (edges[:-1] + edges[1:])
    width = edges[1] - edges[0]
    # Probability assigned to each bucket = area of the true density over it.
    probs = true.cdf(edges[1:]) - true.cdf(edges[:-1])

    axT = axes[0, col]
    axT.bar(centers, probs, width=width * 0.9, color=FILL, edgecolor="white")
    axT.set_title(f"{K} buckets:  $P(\\mathrm{{bucket}})$", fontsize=10)
    axT.set_ylim(0, 0.36)
    if col == 0:
        axT.set_ylabel("bucket probability")

    axB = axes[1, col]
    axB.bar(centers, probs / width, width=width * 0.9, color=FILL, edgecolor="white",
            label="prob / width")
    axB.plot(xgrid, dens, color=LINE, lw=2.0, label="limiting density $f$")
    axB.set_ylim(0, 0.30)
    axB.set_xlabel("wait $x$ (min)")
    if col == 0:
        axB.set_ylabel("prob per unit width")
    if col == 2:
        axB.legend(fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)

plt.tight_layout()
```

The lesson of @fig-melt is the whole intuition in one image. As we slice the continuum
finer, **the probability of any one slice goes to zero** (top row) — this is the
$P(X=x)=0$ phenomenon arriving as a limit. But **the probability *per unit width* settles
down to a fixed curve** (bottom row). That curve is the density. The density is the part of
the discrete picture that is stable under refinement; the bucket probabilities are the part
that evaporates.

Now the second picture, the one we compute with. There are two functions, and the
fundamental theorem of calculus is the bridge between them:

- The **CDF** $F(x)=P(X\le x)$ is the *running total*: how much probability has piled up by
  the time we reach $x$. It starts at $0$ far to the left, climbs to $1$ far to the right,
  and never decreases.
- The **density** $f(x)$ is the *rate of climb* of that running total — its slope,
  $f(x)=F'(x)$. Where $F$ rises steeply, probability is piling up fast, so the density is
  high. Where $F$ is flat, no probability is accruing, so the density is zero.

@fig-cdf-pdf draws both for one distribution, stacked, so the slope-and-area relationship is
visible at a glance.

```{python}
#| label: fig-cdf-pdf
#| fig-cap: 'The two functions and the one relationship between them, for a single continuous distribution. **Top:** the density $f(x)$. The shaded area between $a=2$ and $b=5$ is the probability $P(2\\le X\\le 5)$. **Bottom:** the CDF $F(x)$, the running total of that area from the left. The density is the *slope* of the CDF at each point (steep CDF $\\Rightarrow$ tall density); the probability of the interval is the *rise* of the CDF across it, $F(b)-F(a)$, marked by the bracket. Same number, read two ways: area under $f$, or rise of $F$.'
dist = stats.gamma(a=3.0, scale=1.4)
x = np.linspace(0, 14, 600)
a, b = 2.0, 5.0

fig, (axf, axF) = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(6.6, 5.4), sharex=True)

axf.plot(x, dist.pdf(x), color=LINE, label="density $f(x)$")
mask = (x >= a) & (x <= b)
axf.fill_between(x[mask], dist.pdf(x[mask]), color=FILL, alpha=0.9)
axf.text(3.3, 0.06, r"area $=P(2\le X\le 5)$", fontsize=9, ha="center")
axf.set_ylabel("$f(x)$"); axf.set_title("Density: probability is the shaded area")
axf.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

axF.plot(x, dist.cdf(x), color=POINT, label="CDF $F(x)$")
Fa, Fb = dist.cdf(a), dist.cdf(b)
axF.plot([a, a], [0, Fa], color=GREY, lw=1.0, ls=":")
axF.plot([b, b], [0, Fb], color=GREY, lw=1.0, ls=":")
axF.annotate("", xy=(b + 0.4, Fb), xytext=(b + 0.4, Fa),
             arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="<->", color=ACCENT, lw=1.6))
axF.text(b + 0.7, 0.5 * (Fa + Fb), r"$F(b)-F(a)$", color=ACCENT, fontsize=9, va="center")
axF.set_ylim(0, 1.05)
axF.set_xlabel("$x$"); axF.set_ylabel("$F(x)$")
axF.set_title("CDF: probability is the rise of the running total")
axF.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9, loc="lower right")
plt.tight_layout()
```

With this picture in hand, the formal development is short: define the density as the
derivative of the CDF, invoke the fundamental theorem to turn it back into area, and read
off the three properties. The pictures above are the intuition; the next section is the
proof.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## What "continuous" means, precisely {#sec-def-continuous}

We first pin down the class of variables this article is about. From [PROB-02] we carry the
CDF and its universal properties, which we restate so the chain is explicit.

::: {#def-cdf}
**Definition (cumulative distribution function).** For any random variable $X$, the
**cumulative distribution function** is
$$F(x)=P(X\le x),\qquad x\in\mathbb{R}.$$
It satisfies, for every random variable: (i) it is *non-decreasing*; (ii)
$\lim_{x\to-\infty}F(x)=0$ and $\lim_{x\to+\infty}F(x)=1$; (iii) it is *right-continuous*.
These three are derived from Kolmogorov's axioms in [PROB-02].
:::

A discrete variable's CDF is a *staircase*: flat, then a jump of height $p(x)$ at each value
$x$ the variable can take. The continuous case is the opposite extreme: no jumps at all.

::: {#def-continuous-rv}
**Definition (continuous random variable).** A random variable $X$ is **(absolutely)
continuous** if its CDF $F$ can be written as the integral of some non-negative function
$f$:
$$F(x)=\int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\,dt \qquad\text{for every } x\in\mathbb{R}.$$
Any such $f$ is called a **probability density function** (pdf) of $X$.
:::

The qualifier "absolutely" flags that this is slightly stronger than "$F$ has no jumps"; the
gap between the two is a measure-theoretic subtlety (there exist exotic CDFs that are
continuous but have no density — the Cantor-type "singular" distributions), and it lives
above our floor. We flag it here and move on: every distribution this series uses in
practice is absolutely continuous and has a density. Holding to @def-continuous-rv keeps us
on solid Calc-1 ground, because it makes the density a derivative, as we now show.

::: {.callout-note title="What we are importing, and where it is built"}
We borrow one fact from [PROB-00]/[PROB-02]: that $F(x)=P(X\le x)$ is a legitimate
probability of the event $\{X\le x\}$ and obeys the axioms. Everything else in this article
is built here from that fact plus Calculus I. The measure-theoretic notion of "absolutely
continuous" is named, not assumed; we use only its consequence — that $F$ is an integral of
$f$ — which is all the fundamental theorem needs.
:::

## The density is the derivative of the CDF {#sec-ftc}

@def-continuous-rv writes $F$ as an integral of $f$. The **fundamental theorem of calculus**
— the one genuinely load-bearing tool from the floor — runs that relationship backward and
tells us how to *find* $f$ from $F$.

Recall the theorem in the exact form we need (this is Calc-1, stated so nothing is implicit).
If a function $F$ is built as a running integral of a continuous integrand,
$F(x)=\int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\,dt$, then $F$ is differentiable wherever $f$ is continuous,
and its derivative recovers the integrand:
$$\frac{d}{dx}\,F(x)=\frac{d}{dx}\int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\,dt = f(x).$$
The intuition is the running-total/rate picture from @sec-intuition: the derivative of "area
accumulated up to $x$" is the height of the curve at $x$, because pushing the right endpoint
an extra $dx$ adds a sliver of area of height $f(x)$ and width $dx$, i.e. $f(x)\,dx$, so the
rate of area gain is $f(x)$.

This gives the practical recipe and our central identity.

::: {#thm-pdf-is-derivative}
**Theorem (density as derivative).** If $X$ is continuous with CDF $F$, then at every point
$x$ where $F$ is differentiable, a density is given by
$$f(x)=F'(x).$$
:::

::: {.proof}
By @def-continuous-rv, $F(x)=\int_{-\infty}^{x}f(t)\,dt$ for some non-negative $f$.
Differentiating both sides with respect to $x$ and applying the fundamental theorem of
calculus to the right-hand side gives $F'(x)=f(x)$ at every $x$ where $f$ is continuous (so
that the fundamental theorem applies). $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

A word on the phrase "*a* density" rather than "*the* density." Because changing $f$ at a
handful of isolated points does not change any integral $\int_a^b f$, the density is not
unique at individual points — but it *is* unique up to such harmless edits, and at every
point where $F$ is differentiable, @thm-pdf-is-derivative pins the value down. In practice we
always quote the natural, continuous version.

## Probabilities are areas: the master formula {#sec-area}

Now we close the loop and recover what the whole apparatus was for — computing the
probability that $X$ lands in an interval. This is the formula in the Core box, and it
follows in two lines.

::: {#thm-interval}
**Theorem (interval probability).** If $X$ is continuous with density $f$, then for any
$a\le b$,
$$P(a\le X\le b)=\int_a^b f(x)\,dx.$$
:::

::: {.proof}
The event $\{X\le b\}$ is the disjoint union of $\{X\le a\}$ and $\{a<X\le b\}$ (every
outcome with $X\le b$ either has $X\le a$ or has $a<X\le b$, never both). By the additivity
of probability over disjoint events ([PROB-00]),
$$P(X\le b)=P(X\le a)+P(a<X\le b),$$
so, rearranging and using the definition of $F$,
$$P(a<X\le b)=P(X\le b)-P(X\le a)=F(b)-F(a).$$
Now write each $F$ as the integral from @def-continuous-rv and combine them. Because
$\int_{-\infty}^{b}=\int_{-\infty}^{a}+\int_{a}^{b}$ for any integrand, their difference is
$$F(b)-F(a)=\int_{-\infty}^{b}f(t)\,dt-\int_{-\infty}^{a}f(t)\,dt=\int_a^b f(t)\,dt.$$
This proves $P(a<X\le b)=\int_a^b f$. To replace the strict "$a<$" with the inclusive
"$a\le$", we use the next result, @thm-point-zero, that adding the single endpoint $a$ adds
probability $0$; hence $P(a\le X\le b)=P(a<X\le b)=\int_a^b f$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

So the master computation for a continuous variable is always the same: **to find the
probability of an interval, integrate the density over it.** Probabilities are areas under
$f$.

## Why each value has probability zero — now rigorous {#sec-point-zero}

We argued informally in @sec-why that single values carry no probability. With
@thm-interval in hand, it becomes a one-line calculation.

::: {#thm-point-zero}
**Theorem (point probabilities vanish).** If $X$ is continuous with density $f$, then for
every value $c$,
$$P(X=c)=0.$$
:::

::: {.proof}
The event $\{X=c\}$ is the interval $[c,c]$. By @thm-interval (the same area formula, with
$a=b=c$),
$$P(X=c)=P(c\le X\le c)=\int_c^c f(x)\,dx = 0,$$
because the integral of any function over an interval of zero width is zero (the area of a
region with no horizontal extent is zero). $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is the wire-mass picture made exact: a single point has zero width, so the area above
it — the probability it catches — is zero, no matter how tall $f$ is there.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"For a continuous variable, does it matter whether an inequality is strict or not — is
$P(X<a)$ different from $P(X\le a)$?"* No. The two differ only by the endpoint event
$\{X=a\}$, which by @thm-point-zero has probability $0$. So for a continuous variable all
four of $P(a<X<b)$, $P(a\le X<b)$, $P(a<X\le b)$, $P(a\le X\le b)$ are *equal*. (This is
emphatically **false** for discrete variables, where the endpoints carry real mass — a
favorite trap. Whenever you see endpoints treated casually, check first that the variable is
continuous.)
:::

## The two laws every density obeys {#sec-density-laws}

A function does not get to be a density just because we call it one. Exactly two conditions
qualify it, and both come straight from the axioms via the picture above.

::: {#thm-valid-density}
**Theorem (the density conditions).** A function $f$ is the density of some continuous
random variable if and only if
$$\text{(non-negativity)}\quad f(x)\ge 0 \text{ for all } x,
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\text{(normalization)}\quad \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1.$$
:::

::: {.proof}
*Non-negativity is necessary.* The density is the slope of the CDF, $f=F'$, and $F$ is
non-decreasing (a probability $P(X\le x)$ cannot shrink as $x$ grows, by monotonicity in
[PROB-02]). The slope of a non-decreasing function is $\ge 0$ everywhere it exists, so
$f\ge 0$.

*Normalization is necessary.* The total area under $f$ is the probability that $X$ lands
*somewhere*, which is the probability of the whole line — a certain event. Concretely,
taking the limit $b\to\infty$, $a\to-\infty$ in @thm-interval and using the CDF's limits
from @def-cdf,
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx=\lim_{b\to\infty}F(b)-\lim_{a\to-\infty}F(a)=1-0=1.$$

*The two conditions are sufficient.* Conversely, given any $f\ge 0$ with
$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}f=1$, define $F(x)=\int_{-\infty}^x f(t)\,dt$. We check the three
CDF properties of @def-cdf one at a time. (i) $F$ is **non-decreasing**: for $x_1\le x_2$,
$F(x_2)-F(x_1)=\int_{x_1}^{x_2} f(t)\,dt\ge 0$ because we are integrating a non-negative
function over an interval, so adding the extra slice of area can only raise the running
total. (ii) The **limits** are right: as $x\to-\infty$ the region of integration shrinks to
nothing, so $F(x)\to 0$; as $x\to+\infty$ the integral fills out to the full area, which is
$1$ by normalization. (iii) $F$ is **continuous** (indeed more than the right-continuity the
axioms demand): nudging the upper limit from $x$ to $x+h$ changes $F$ by
$\int_x^{x+h} f(t)\,dt$, the area of a sliver of width $h$, which shrinks to $0$ as $h\to 0$
— so $F$ has no jumps. All three properties hold, so $F$ is a valid CDF, and since
$F(x)=\int_{-\infty}^x f$ it is (absolutely) continuous with density $f$ by
@def-continuous-rv. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

These are the *only* two requirements. Notice what is conspicuously **absent**: there is no
requirement that $f(x)\le 1$. We make this its own headline because it is the single most
common misconception about densities.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**A density is not a probability, and may exceed 1.** Probabilities live in $[0,1]$;
densities do not. A density is *probability per unit length*, a rate. Squeeze the same total
probability of $1$ into a narrow interval and the density must rise high to keep the area at
$1$ — exactly as a fixed mass squeezed into a short wire forces a high mass-per-centimeter.
For the uniform distribution on $[0,\tfrac12]$ (next section), the density is constant at
$f(x)=2$ — twice the forbidden ceiling, and perfectly legal, because the area is
$2\times\tfrac12=1$. If you ever find yourself "worried" that a density came out bigger than
$1$, you have confused the rate with the quantity. Only after integrating do you get a number
that must sit in $[0,1]$.
:::

This pitfall *is* the why of the article restated: every property of $f$ follows from "$f$ is
a rate, not an amount." Non-negativity (you cannot accumulate negative probability), area one
(the total amount is certain), and the freedom to exceed $1$ (a rate has no ceiling) are all
the same idea seen three ways.

## A note on the "infinitesimal probability" reading {#sec-infinitesimal}

There is one more reading of $f$ that practitioners use constantly and that makes the
density tangible. From @thm-interval, the probability in a *tiny* interval of width $dx$
around a point $x$ is approximately the density times the width:
$$P\!\left(x\le X\le x+dx\right)=\int_x^{x+dx} f(t)\,dt \approx f(x)\,dx,$$
because over a sliver so thin that $f$ barely changes, the area under the curve is just
height $\times$ width, $f(x)\,dx$. (Formally this is the Calc-1 statement that
$\lim_{dx\to0}\frac{1}{dx}\int_x^{x+dx}f = f(x)$, the same fundamental theorem again.) So
the density answers the question "**roughly how much probability sits in a small window
here, per unit of window width?**" That is the operational meaning of $f(x)$, and it is why
$f(x)$ is the right thing to compare across points: $f(x_1)=2f(x_2)$ says a small window at
$x_1$ is about twice as likely to catch $X$ as an equally small window at $x_2$.

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run the machinery on three real, honest distributions, building in difficulty.

## Example 1 — the uniform: the bus you have no information about {#sec-uniform}

You arrive at a stop served by a shuttle that comes *exactly* every $30$ minutes, but you
have no idea where you are in its cycle — you might have just missed one, or one might be
pulling in. With no information favoring any moment over another, the time $X$ (in minutes)
until the next shuttle is **uniform** on $[0,30]$: every sub-interval of equal length is
equally likely. This is the honest habitat of the uniform — *maximal ignorance over a
bounded range* — and it is the simplest density there is.

"Equally likely over $[0,30]$" forces a *constant* density on that interval and zero
outside. Call the constant $c$. Normalization (@thm-valid-density) fixes it: the area is the
constant times the width,
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f = \int_0^{30} c\,dx = c\cdot 30 = 1
\;\Longrightarrow\; c=\frac{1}{30}.$$
So the density is
$$f(x)=\begin{cases}\dfrac{1}{30}, & 0\le x\le 30,\\[4pt] 0,&\text{otherwise.}\end{cases}$$
In general, $\mathrm{Uniform}(\alpha,\beta)$ has density $f(x)=\tfrac{1}{\beta-\alpha}$ on
$[\alpha,\beta]$. Note $\tfrac{1}{30}$ is below $1$ here only because the interval is wide;
on $[0,\tfrac12]$ the same logic gives $f=2$, the legal "density above one" from the pitfall.

Now everything else is areas of rectangles. The CDF accumulates the constant density:
$$F(x)=\int_0^x \frac{1}{30}\,dt = \frac{x}{30}\quad\text{for } 0\le x\le 30,$$
a straight ramp from $0$ to $1$. The probability you wait *between $5$ and $15$ minutes* is
the area of a rectangle of height $\tfrac1{30}$ and width $10$:
$$P(5\le X\le 15)=\int_5^{15}\frac{1}{30}\,dx = \frac{15-5}{30}=\frac{10}{30}=\frac13.$$
@fig-uniform draws the density, the CDF, and this probability as an area.

```{python}
#| label: fig-uniform
#| fig-cap: 'The uniform shuttle-wait on $[0,30]$. **Left:** the density is a flat plateau at height $1/30\\approx0.033$; the shaded rectangle is $P(5\\le X\\le 15)=10/30=1/3$ — area = height $\\times$ width. **Right:** the CDF is the straight ramp $F(x)=x/30$, and the same probability is its rise $F(15)-F(5)=1/3$ (bracket). The dots show $n=2000$ simulated waits binned into a histogram *scaled to a density* (probability per unit width); it hugs the flat line, confirming the density is the right per-width object to compare against.'
alpha, beta = 0.0, 30.0
c = 1.0 / (beta - alpha)
x = np.linspace(-3, 33, 600)
f = np.where((x >= alpha) & (x <= beta), c, 0.0)
F = np.clip((x - alpha) / (beta - alpha), 0, 1)

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
sample = rng.uniform(alpha, beta, size=2000)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7))
axL.hist(sample, bins=20, range=(0, 30), density=True, color=FILL, alpha=0.55,
         edgecolor="white", label="simulated (density-scaled)")
axL.plot(x, f, color=LINE, label="density $f$")
m = (x >= 5) & (x <= 15)
axL.fill_between(x[m], f[m], color=ACCENT, alpha=0.35)
axL.text(10, 0.016, r"$P(5\le X\le 15)=\frac13$", ha="center", fontsize=9)
axL.set_xlabel("wait $x$ (min)"); axL.set_ylabel("density"); axL.set_title("Uniform density")
axL.legend(fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9, loc="upper right")

axR.plot(x, F, color=POINT, label="CDF $F(x)=x/30$")
F5, F15 = 5 / 30, 15 / 30
axR.plot([5, 5], [0, F5], ":", color=GREY); axR.plot([15, 15], [0, F15], ":", color=GREY)
axR.annotate("", xy=(16.5, F15), xytext=(16.5, F5),
             arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="<->", color=ACCENT, lw=1.6))
axR.text(17.5, 0.5 * (F5 + F15), r"$\frac13$", color=ACCENT, fontsize=10, va="center")
axR.set_xlabel("wait $x$ (min)"); axR.set_ylabel("$F(x)$"); axR.set_title("Uniform CDF")
axR.set_ylim(0, 1.05); axR.legend(fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9, loc="lower right")
plt.tight_layout()
```

The histogram in @fig-uniform is the single most important habit for *reading* a density off
data: you must scale the histogram to a **density** (`density=True`: bar height = fraction of
data in the bar $\div$ bar width), not to raw counts, before it is comparable to $f$.
A count histogram changes height when you change the number of bars; a density histogram does
not, because — exactly like $f$ — it measures probability *per unit width*.

## Example 2 — a triangular density: when extremes are less likely {#sec-triangular}

The uniform says "all values equally likely," which is rarely true. A small but honest step
up is the **triangular** distribution, the standard tool when you have a plausible *minimum*,
*maximum*, and *most-likely* value but little else — the classic case being a project-time or
cost estimate elicited from an expert ("at best 2 weeks, at worst 8, most likely 4"). The
density rises linearly to a peak at the mode, then falls linearly to the max, making
middling values likeliest and extremes rare. We build it from scratch to exercise the density
laws on a non-constant $f$.

Take a task whose duration $X$ (weeks) has minimum $a=2$, mode $m=4$, maximum $b=8$. The
density is two line segments meeting at the peak $(m, h)$ and hitting zero at $a$ and $b$:
$$f(x)=\begin{cases}
\text{a line from } (a,0) \text{ to } (m,h), & a\le x\le m,\\
\text{a line from } (m,h) \text{ to } (b,0), & m\le x\le b,\\
0,&\text{otherwise.}
\end{cases}$$
The peak height $h$ is *not* free — normalization fixes it. The region under the density is a
triangle with base $b-a$ and height $h$, and the area of a triangle is
$\tfrac12\,\text{base}\times\text{height}$:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f = \tfrac12 (b-a)\,h = 1
\;\Longrightarrow\; h=\frac{2}{b-a}=\frac{2}{8-2}=\frac13.$$
Writing the two line segments explicitly (each is "height $h$ scaled by how far $x$ has
travelled along its segment"):
$$f(x)=\begin{cases}
\dfrac{2(x-a)}{(b-a)(m-a)}, & a\le x\le m,\\[8pt]
\dfrac{2(b-x)}{(b-a)(b-m)}, & m< x\le b.
\end{cases}$$
You can confirm $f(m)=\tfrac{2(m-a)}{(b-a)(m-a)}=\tfrac{2}{b-a}=h$, the peak we found, so the
pieces meet. Let us compute a probability that needs a real integral, not a rectangle:
$P(X\le 3)$, the chance the task finishes within three weeks. Since $3$ is on the rising
segment ($a=2\le3\le m=4$), we integrate the first piece from $a=2$ to $3$:
$$P(X\le 3)=\int_2^3 \frac{2(x-2)}{(8-2)(4-2)}\,dx
=\frac{2}{6\cdot 2}\int_2^3 (x-2)\,dx
=\frac{1}{6}\left[\frac{(x-2)^2}{2}\right]_2^3
=\frac{1}{6}\cdot\frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{12}\approx 0.083.$$
Step by step: we pulled the constant $\tfrac{2}{12}=\tfrac16$ out of the integral; the
antiderivative of $(x-2)$ is $\tfrac{(x-2)^2}{2}$ (a Calc-1 power rule on the shifted
variable); evaluating from $2$ to $3$ gives $\tfrac{1^2}{2}-\tfrac{0^2}{2}=\tfrac12$; times
$\tfrac16$ is $\tfrac1{12}$. So there is only about an $8.3\%$ chance the task finishes in
three weeks — the rising edge of the triangle is short and low. @fig-triangular shows the
density, this area, and the resulting CDF (now a *curve*, because the density is not
constant).

```{python}
#| label: fig-triangular
#| fig-cap: 'A triangular duration model with min $2$, mode $4$, max $8$ weeks. **Left:** the density peaks at the mode (height $h=1/3$) and falls to zero at the endpoints; the shaded sliver is $P(X\\le 3)=1/12\\approx0.083$. The density now varies with $x$, so probabilities are genuine integrals (areas of triangles/trapezoids), not just rectangles. **Right:** the CDF is now *curved* — concave up on the rising density, concave down on the falling density — because the rate of probability accumulation itself changes. Simulated draws (density-scaled histogram) track the triangle.'
a, m, b = 2.0, 4.0, 8.0
h = 2.0 / (b - a)

def tri_pdf(x):
    x = np.asarray(x, float)
    up = 2 * (x - a) / ((b - a) * (m - a))
    dn = 2 * (b - x) / ((b - a) * (b - m))
    return np.where((x >= a) & (x <= m), up,
           np.where((x > m) & (x <= b), dn, 0.0))

def tri_cdf(x):
    x = np.asarray(x, float)
    left = (x - a) ** 2 / ((b - a) * (m - a))
    right = 1 - (b - x) ** 2 / ((b - a) * (b - m))
    return np.where(x < a, 0.0,
           np.where(x <= m, left,
           np.where(x <= b, right, 1.0)))

xs = np.linspace(0, 10, 700)
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
sample = rng.triangular(a, m, b, size=4000)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7))
axL.hist(sample, bins=28, range=(2, 8), density=True, color=FILL, alpha=0.55,
         edgecolor="white", label="simulated")
axL.plot(xs, tri_pdf(xs), color=LINE, label="density $f$")
mm = (xs >= a) & (xs <= 3)
axL.fill_between(xs[mm], tri_pdf(xs[mm]), color=ACCENT, alpha=0.4)
axL.text(2.9, 0.02, r"$P(X\le 3)$", fontsize=9)
axL.set_xlabel("duration $x$ (weeks)"); axL.set_ylabel("density")
axL.set_title("Triangular density"); axL.legend(fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)

axR.plot(xs, tri_cdf(xs), color=POINT, label="CDF $F$")
axR.scatter([3], [tri_cdf(3)], color=ACCENT, zorder=4, s=40)
axR.annotate(r"$F(3)=\frac{1}{12}$", (3, tri_cdf(3)), textcoords="offset points",
             xytext=(12, -4), fontsize=9, color=ACCENT)
axR.set_xlabel("duration $x$ (weeks)"); axR.set_ylabel("$F(x)$")
axR.set_title("Triangular CDF (curved)"); axR.set_ylim(0, 1.05)
axR.legend(fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9, loc="lower right")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Given a density, how do you get the CDF, and given the CDF, how do you get the density?"*
The two are inverse operations and you should be fluent in both directions. **Density
$\to$ CDF: integrate** (accumulate from the left), $F(x)=\int_{-\infty}^x f$. **CDF $\to$
density: differentiate**, $f=F'$ (@thm-pdf-is-derivative). The triangular shows the round
trip: we integrated the line $f$ to get the parabola-arc $F$, and differentiating $F$ would
return the line. *Probabilities of intervals always come from the CDF differences
$F(b)-F(a)$* — which is why having $F$ in closed form, as here, is so convenient.
:::

## Example 3 — the exponential: memoryless waiting at the edge of this article {#sec-exponential}

The third example is the **exponential** distribution, the natural model for the *waiting
time until a random event* — a radioactive decay, a server request, a customer arrival —
when the event's "hazard" is constant in time. It is also our deliberate **edge case**: it
is the first density with *unbounded* support (it stretches to $+\infty$), it requires an
*improper* integral to normalize, and it has a famous, slightly unsettling property
(memorylessness) that exposes both the power and the honest limits of the continuous model.
The exponential gets its full treatment in [PROB-14]; here we use it to stress-test
everything above.

The exponential with **rate** $\lambda>0$ has density
$$f(x)=\begin{cases}\lambda e^{-\lambda x}, & x\ge 0,\\ 0,& x<0,\end{cases}$$
defined only for non-negative times (you cannot wait a negative amount). First, **does it
even normalize?** The support is infinite, so this is an *improper integral*, handled the
Calc-1 way — as a limit of proper integrals:
$$\int_0^{\infty}\lambda e^{-\lambda x}\,dx
=\lim_{T\to\infty}\int_0^{T}\lambda e^{-\lambda x}\,dx
=\lim_{T\to\infty}\Bigl[-e^{-\lambda x}\Bigr]_0^{T}
=\lim_{T\to\infty}\bigl(-e^{-\lambda T}+e^{0}\bigr)
=0+1=1.$$
Walking it: the antiderivative of $\lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ is $-e^{-\lambda x}$ (check by
differentiating: $\frac{d}{dx}(-e^{-\lambda x})=-(-\lambda)e^{-\lambda x}=\lambda
e^{-\lambda x}$, using the chain rule on the exponent); evaluating from $0$ to $T$ gives
$-e^{-\lambda T}+1$; and as $T\to\infty$ the term $e^{-\lambda T}\to 0$ because the exponent
$-\lambda T\to-\infty$. The area is exactly $1$, so $f$ is a valid density. The CDF is the
same integral with a variable upper limit:
$$F(x)=\int_0^x \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \bigl[-e^{-\lambda t}\bigr]_0^x
=1-e^{-\lambda x}\quad(x\ge0).$$

Make it concrete. Customer-support calls arrive at a small desk at a rate of $\lambda=0.5$
per minute on average, so inter-arrival times are (modeled as) exponential with
$\lambda=0.5$. The probability the next call comes **within 2 minutes** is
$$P(X\le 2)=F(2)=1-e^{-0.5\cdot 2}=1-e^{-1}\approx 1-0.368=0.632.$$
And the probability you wait **more than 4 minutes** is the tail
$$P(X>4)=1-F(4)=e^{-0.5\cdot 4}=e^{-2}\approx 0.135.$$
@fig-exponential draws the density, this tail, and the way the distribution stretches to
infinity while still enclosing total area $1$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-exponential
#| fig-cap: 'The exponential inter-arrival model, rate $\\lambda=0.5$/min. **Left:** the density $\\lambda e^{-\\lambda x}$ starts at its highest value $f(0)=\\lambda=0.5$ and decays; the shaded right tail is $P(X>4)=e^{-2}\\approx0.135$. The support runs to $+\\infty$, yet the total area is exactly 1 — an *improper* integral that converges. **Right:** the CDF $1-e^{-\\lambda x}$ rises fast then levels off toward (never reaching) 1, the signature of an unbounded-support distribution. Density-scaled histogram of $n=5000$ simulated waits overlaid.'
lam = 0.5
xs = np.linspace(0, 14, 700)
f = lam * np.exp(-lam * xs)
F = 1 - np.exp(-lam * xs)
rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
sample = rng.exponential(scale=1 / lam, size=5000)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7))
axL.hist(sample, bins=40, range=(0, 14), density=True, color=FILL, alpha=0.55,
         edgecolor="white", label="simulated")
axL.plot(xs, f, color=LINE, label=r"density $\lambda e^{-\lambda x}$")
tail = xs >= 4
axL.fill_between(xs[tail], f[tail], color=ACCENT, alpha=0.4)
axL.text(5.4, 0.05, r"$P(X>4)=e^{-2}$", fontsize=9)
axL.set_xlabel("wait $x$ (min)"); axL.set_ylabel("density")
axL.set_title("Exponential density"); axL.legend(fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)

axR.plot(xs, F, color=POINT, label=r"CDF $1-e^{-\lambda x}$")
axR.axhline(1, color=GREY, ls=":", lw=1.0)
axR.set_xlabel("wait $x$ (min)"); axR.set_ylabel("$F(x)$")
axR.set_title("Exponential CDF"); axR.set_ylim(0, 1.05)
axR.legend(fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9, loc="lower right")
plt.tight_layout()
```

Why is this the *edge*? Because the exponential's defining feature is **memorylessness**:
given that you have already waited $s$ minutes with no call, the probability of waiting at
least $t$ more is the *same* as the original probability of waiting at least $t$ — the
process "forgets" how long you have been waiting. We can show it directly from $F$, using the
conditional-probability definition from [PROB-01], $P(A\mid B)=P(A\cap B)/P(B)$:
$$P(X>s+t \mid X>s)=\frac{P(X>s+t \text{ and } X>s)}{P(X>s)}
=\frac{P(X>s+t)}{P(X>s)}
=\frac{e^{-\lambda(s+t)}}{e^{-\lambda s}}
=e^{-\lambda t}=P(X>t).$$
The middle step uses $\{X>s+t\}\subseteq\{X>s\}$, so their intersection is just
$\{X>s+t\}$; the rest is the tail formula $P(X>x)=e^{-\lambda x}$ and the exponent law
$e^{-\lambda(s+t)}/e^{-\lambda s}=e^{-\lambda t}$. This is mathematically beautiful and
physically *suspicious*: it says a component that has run for $1000$ hours is exactly as
likely to fail in the next hour as a brand-new one. For many real systems (machines that
wear, customers who get impatient) that is plainly false — which is precisely why the
exponential is an edge case and the more flexible **gamma/Weibull** families ([PROB-14])
exist to relax it. The honesty rule of this series: the exponential is a superb model
*exactly when* the constant-hazard assumption holds (radioactive decay, idealized Poisson
arrivals) and a misleading one when it does not.

# Transforming a continuous variable: change of variables {#sec-change-of-vars}

A question that arises constantly: we know the density of $X$, and we form a new variable
$Y=g(X)$ — converting Celsius to Fahrenheit, seconds to a rate, a measurement to its
logarithm. **What is the density of $Y$?** The naive guess "just substitute" is wrong, and
seeing *why* it is wrong is the entire content of the change-of-variables rule. This is the
scalar, Calc-1 shadow of the multivariate Jacobian formula in [PROB-18]; we build it here
with single-variable substitution only.

## Deriving the rule through the CDF {#sec-cov-derivation}

The reliable method — the one that never lies — is to **transform the CDF, then
differentiate**, never to transform the density directly. We do the increasing case in full,
then the decreasing case, then unify.

Let $X$ have density $f_X$ and CDF $F_X$, and suppose $g$ is **strictly increasing** and
differentiable (so it has a differentiable inverse $g^{-1}$). Define $Y=g(X)$. We want
$f_Y$. Start, as always, from the CDF of the new variable — the one universal object — and
unwind the transform:
$$F_Y(y)=P(Y\le y)=P\bigl(g(X)\le y\bigr).$$
Because $g$ is strictly increasing, applying its inverse to both sides of "$g(X)\le y$"
preserves the inequality (increasing functions keep order): $g(X)\le y$ exactly when
$X\le g^{-1}(y)$. So
$$F_Y(y)=P\bigl(X\le g^{-1}(y)\bigr)=F_X\bigl(g^{-1}(y)\bigr).$$
Now differentiate with respect to $y$ to get the density, $f_Y=F_Y'$. The right-hand side is
a composition, so we use the **chain rule** (the Calc-1 tool, stated:
$\frac{d}{dy}H(u(y))=H'(u(y))\,u'(y)$) with $H=F_X$ and $u=g^{-1}$. Since $F_X'=f_X$,
$$f_Y(y)=\frac{d}{dy}F_X\bigl(g^{-1}(y)\bigr)
=f_X\bigl(g^{-1}(y)\bigr)\cdot \frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y).$$
The factor $\frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y)$ is the derivative of the inverse — the *stretch factor* of
the transform — and for an increasing $g$ it is positive. That is the rule, for increasing
$g$.

Now the **strictly decreasing** case, because it carries a crucial sign. If $g$ is
decreasing, applying its inverse to "$g(X)\le y$" *flips* the inequality (decreasing
functions reverse order): $g(X)\le y$ exactly when $X\ge g^{-1}(y)$. So now
$$F_Y(y)=P\bigl(X\ge g^{-1}(y)\bigr)=1-F_X\bigl(g^{-1}(y)\bigr),$$
using $P(X\ge c)=1-P(X<c)=1-F_X(c)$ (the last equality because the single point $c$ has
probability zero, @thm-point-zero). Differentiating, the leading $1$ dies and the chain rule
gives a minus sign:
$$f_Y(y)=-\,f_X\bigl(g^{-1}(y)\bigr)\cdot\frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y).$$
But for a decreasing $g$, the inverse $g^{-1}$ is also decreasing, so its derivative
$\frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y)$ is *negative* — and the leading minus sign makes the whole product
positive, as a density must be. The increasing and decreasing cases differ only by which
sign keeps $f_Y\ge0$, so we can fold them into one formula using absolute value, which is
exactly what packages "take whichever sign makes the stretch factor positive."

::: {#thm-change-of-vars}
**Theorem (change of variables, monotone scalar transform).** Let $X$ be continuous with
density $f_X$, and let $g$ be a strictly monotone, differentiable function on the range of
$X$, with inverse $x=g^{-1}(y)$. Then $Y=g(X)$ has density
$$f_Y(y)=f_X\bigl(g^{-1}(y)\bigr)\,\left|\frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y)\right|
= \frac{f_X\bigl(g^{-1}(y)\bigr)}{\bigl|g'\!\bigl(g^{-1}(y)\bigr)\bigr|},$$
for $y$ in the range of $g$ (and $f_Y(y)=0$ elsewhere).
:::

::: {.proof}
The increasing and decreasing derivations above each produced
$f_Y(y)=f_X(g^{-1}(y))\cdot(\pm)\frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y)$ with the sign chosen so the result is
non-negative; that is exactly $f_X(g^{-1}(y))\,\bigl|\frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y)\bigr|$. The second
form uses the **inverse-function derivative rule** from Calc 1,
$\frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y)=\dfrac{1}{g'\!\bigl(g^{-1}(y)\bigr)}$ (which holds because
differentiating the identity $g(g^{-1}(y))=y$ by the chain rule gives
$g'(g^{-1}(y))\cdot\frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y)=1$). Taking absolute values gives the stated form.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The factor $\bigl|\frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y)\bigr|$ — the **Jacobian** of the one-variable
transform — is the heart of the matter and the reason "just substitute" fails. It accounts
for how $g$ *stretches or compresses* the axis. If $g$ spreads a small interval of $x$ into a
wide interval of $y$, the same probability is smeared over more width, so the density must
*drop* there — and indeed the stretch factor is large, dividing the density down. Density is
probability per unit width, so when the width changes, the density must change to keep the
*area* (the actual probability) invariant. This is the "$f$ is a rate" principle once more,
now governing transforms.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Never get $f_Y$ by just plugging $g^{-1}(y)$ into $f_X$ and stopping.** The substitution
$f_X(g^{-1}(y))$ is only *half* the answer; without the Jacobian factor
$\bigl|\frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y)\bigr|$ the result will not even integrate to $1$. The way to
never make this mistake is to never transform the density directly — always transform the
*CDF* (a probability, which substitution *does* respect) and differentiate at the end, as in
the derivation above. The Jacobian then appears automatically from the chain rule.
:::

## Worked transform: a linear rescaling, and a log {#sec-cov-example}

Two quick applications make the rule concrete.

**Linear rescaling** (units change). Let $X$ be the customer-wait $\mathrm{Exponential}(0.5)$
in *minutes* from @sec-exponential, and let $Y=60X$ be the same wait in *seconds*. Here
$g(x)=60x$, increasing, with inverse $g^{-1}(y)=y/60$ and
$\frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y)=\tfrac1{60}$. By @thm-change-of-vars,
$$f_Y(y)=f_X\!\left(\frac{y}{60}\right)\cdot\frac{1}{60}
=0.5\,e^{-0.5\,(y/60)}\cdot\frac{1}{60}
=\frac{0.5}{60}\,e^{-(0.5/60)\,y}.$$
This is again exponential, now with rate $\lambda/60=0.5/60$ per *second* — exactly right,
since calls arriving at $0.5$/min arrive at $0.5/60$/sec. The Jacobian $\tfrac1{60}$ scaled
the density down by $60$ because seconds spread the same probability over $60\times$ the
width. *Without* the $\tfrac1{60}$ we would have a curve that integrates to $60$, not $1$.

**A logarithm** (the lognormal seed). Suppose a positive quantity is modeled by
$Y=e^{X}$ where $X$ is some continuous variable — the standard way multiplicative,
always-positive quantities (incomes, biological concentrations) are built. Here $g(x)=e^x$,
increasing, inverse $g^{-1}(y)=\ln y$ for $y>0$, with $\frac{d}{dy}\ln y=\tfrac1y$. So
$$f_Y(y)=f_X(\ln y)\cdot\frac{1}{y},\qquad y>0.$$
The Jacobian $\tfrac1y$ is doing visible work: it tilts the density to pile up near small
$y$ and stretch out the large-$y$ tail, which is exactly the right-skewed shape such
quantities have. When $X$ is normal this produces the **lognormal** distribution ([PROB-13],
[PROB-14]). @fig-cov visualizes the log transform turning a symmetric density into a skewed
one, the stretch factor made visible.

```{python}
#| label: fig-cov
#| fig-cap: 'Change of variables, $Y=e^{X}$, visualized. **Left:** the density of $X$ (a symmetric normal, for illustration). **Right:** the density of $Y=e^X$ obtained two ways that agree — the formula $f_Y(y)=f_X(\\ln y)/y$ (red curve) and a density-scaled histogram of $Y$ values from transformed simulated $X$ (bars). The exponential map stretches the right side of the $x$-axis enormously and compresses the left, so the Jacobian $1/y$ pulls the density down for large $y$ (smeared over more width) and up for small $y$ — turning symmetry into right skew. This is why you cannot just substitute: the *shape* changes, governed by the stretch factor.'
from scipy import stats
muX, sdX = 0.0, 0.6
X = stats.norm(muX, sdX)
xs = np.linspace(-2.2, 2.2, 600)

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
xsamp = rng.normal(muX, sdX, size=6000)
ysamp = np.exp(xsamp)

ys = np.linspace(0.05, 6, 600)
fY = X.pdf(np.log(ys)) / ys   # the change-of-variables formula

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7))
axL.plot(xs, X.pdf(xs), color=POINT, label="$f_X$ (symmetric)")
axL.fill_between(xs, X.pdf(xs), color=FILL, alpha=0.4)
axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("density"); axL.set_title("Density of $X$")
axL.legend(fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)

axR.hist(ysamp, bins=60, range=(0, 6), density=True, color=FILL, alpha=0.55,
         edgecolor="white", label="simulated $Y=e^X$")
axR.plot(ys, fY, color=LINE, label=r"$f_Y(y)=f_X(\ln y)/y$")
axR.set_xlabel("$y$"); axR.set_ylabel("density"); axR.set_title("Density of $Y=e^X$ (skewed)")
axR.set_xlim(0, 6); axR.legend(fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What if the transform $g$ is not monotone — say $Y=X^2$?"* Then the clean one-to-one
inverse fails: a given $y>0$ has **two** preimages, $x=+\sqrt{y}$ and $x=-\sqrt{y}$, so the
event $\{Y\le y\}$ is $\{-\sqrt y\le X\le\sqrt y\}$. You still win by going through the CDF.
For $y>0$,
$$F_Y(y)=P(-\sqrt y\le X\le\sqrt y)=F_X(\sqrt y)-F_X(-\sqrt y).$$
Now differentiate with respect to $y$, term by term, with the chain rule (recall
$F_X'=f_X$). The first term: $\frac{d}{dy}F_X(\sqrt y)=f_X(\sqrt y)\cdot\frac{d}{dy}\sqrt y
=f_X(\sqrt y)\cdot\frac{1}{2\sqrt y}$, using $\frac{d}{dy}y^{1/2}=\tfrac12 y^{-1/2}$. The
second term carries a minus sign and an inner derivative
$\frac{d}{dy}(-\sqrt y)=-\frac{1}{2\sqrt y}$, so
$-\frac{d}{dy}F_X(-\sqrt y)=-f_X(-\sqrt y)\cdot\bigl(-\tfrac{1}{2\sqrt y}\bigr)
=+f_X(-\sqrt y)\cdot\frac{1}{2\sqrt y}$ — the two minus signs cancel, leaving a positive
contribution. Adding them,
$$f_Y(y)=\frac{1}{2\sqrt y}\bigl[f_X(\sqrt y)+f_X(-\sqrt y)\bigr],\qquad y>0,$$
a *sum* of two Jacobian terms, one per branch, each of the same $\tfrac{1}{2\sqrt y}$
stretch factor (here $\tfrac{1}{2\sqrt y}$ is $\bigl|\frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}\bigr|$ for each
branch $g^{-1}(y)=\pm\sqrt y$). The general rule for
many-to-one $g$ is "**sum the monotone formula over all branches**." (This is exactly the
construction that turns a standard normal into a chi-square in [PROB-15].) The lesson:
@thm-change-of-vars is the monotone special case; the CDF method handles everything, which is
why we always anchor on the CDF.
:::

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

A density is a powerful object, but several honest caveats decide whether the continuous
model is the right tool. This is the section that separates "can integrate a density" from
"knows when a density is the wrong description."

## The model is continuous; the world is often not {#sec-quantization}

The deepest caveat is conceptual: **no real measurement is truly continuous.** A voltmeter
reports to the millivolt; a stopwatch to the centisecond; money to the cent. Strictly,
every dataset is discrete (it has finitely many representable values), so $P(X=x)=0$ is
literally false for recorded data — the value $4.13$ may well appear twice. The continuous
density is an *idealization*: a smooth stand-in for a finely-quantized discrete reality,
adopted because integrals are far more tractable than sums over thousands of bins and
because the idealization is excellent when the quantization step is tiny relative to the
spread. @fig-quantize shows when the idealization is honest and when it frays.

```{python}
#| label: fig-quantize
#| fig-cap: "When the continuous idealization holds, and when it breaks. A true continuous wait is rounded to a recording grid. **Left (fine grid, 0.1 min):** the rounded data's density-scaled histogram is indistinguishable from the continuous density — the idealization is excellent. **Right (coarse grid, 2 min):** rounding has collapsed the data onto a few spikes; it is now genuinely discrete and a continuous density misrepresents it. The continuous model is honest only when the measurement resolution is fine relative to the distribution's spread."
from scipy import stats
lam = 0.5
true = stats.expon(scale=1 / lam)
xs = np.linspace(0, 12, 500)
rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
raw = rng.exponential(scale=1 / lam, size=8000)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7), sharey=True)
for ax, step, title in ((axL, 0.1, "Fine grid (0.1 min): idealization holds"),
                         (axR, 2.0, "Coarse grid (2 min): now discrete")):
    rounded = np.round(raw / step) * step
    ax.hist(rounded, bins=np.arange(0, 12 + step, max(step, 0.3)), density=True,
            color=FILL, alpha=0.6, edgecolor="white", label="recorded data")
    ax.plot(xs, true.pdf(xs), color=LINE, label="continuous density")
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=9.5); ax.set_xlabel("wait (min)")
    ax.legend(fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)
axL.set_ylabel("density")
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Mixed variables: when a point *does* carry mass {#sec-mixed}

The continuous framework assumes *all* the probability is spread as density. Real variables
often violate this with a single atom. Insurance claims are the canonical case: a policy's
annual payout $X$ is **zero with substantial probability** (most years, no claim) and
*continuous and positive* otherwise. Such an $X$ is neither discrete nor continuous but
**mixed**: its CDF has a jump (at $0$) *and* a smooth rising part. There is no ordinary
density — $P(X=0)>0$ contradicts @thm-point-zero — so the pure-density machinery of this
article does not apply as-is. The honest fix is to model the atom and the continuous part
separately (a "zero-inflated" or two-part model), or to use the CDF directly, which always
exists ([PROB-02]). @fig-mixed shows such a CDF, with its tell-tale jump.

```{python}
#| label: fig-mixed
#| fig-cap: "A mixed variable that no density can describe: annual insurance payout. With probability 0.7 the payout is exactly 0 (no claim); otherwise it is exponential. **Left:** the CDF jumps by 0.7 at $x=0$ (the atom — violating the continuity that gives a density) and then rises smoothly (the continuous part). **Right:** the corresponding 'density picture' must mix a spike (an arrow, carrying probability mass 0.7) with a genuine density curve (carrying the remaining 0.3). This is the boundary of this article: when a point carries mass, fall back to the CDF or a two-part model."
from scipy import stats
p0 = 0.7
lam = 0.4
xs = np.linspace(-1, 12, 700)
# CDF: 0 for x<0; jump of p0 at 0; then p0 + (1-p0)*expon CDF.
cdf = np.where(xs < 0, 0.0, p0 + (1 - p0) * (1 - np.exp(-lam * np.maximum(xs, 0))))

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7))
axL.plot(xs[xs < 0], cdf[xs < 0], color=POINT)
axL.plot(xs[xs >= 0], cdf[xs >= 0], color=POINT)
axL.plot([0, 0], [0, p0], color=POINT, ls=":")
axL.scatter([0], [p0], color=LINE, zorder=4, s=35)
axL.scatter([0], [0], facecolors="white", edgecolors=POINT, zorder=4, s=35)
axL.annotate("jump = 0.7\n(atom at 0)", (0, p0), textcoords="offset points",
             xytext=(18, -6), fontsize=8.5, color=LINE)
axL.set_xlabel("payout $x$"); axL.set_ylabel("$F(x)$"); axL.set_title("Mixed CDF: a jump + a curve")
axL.set_ylim(0, 1.05)

cont = (1 - p0) * lam * np.exp(-lam * np.maximum(xs, 0))
axR.plot(xs[xs >= 0], cont[xs >= 0], color=LINE, label="continuous part (mass 0.3)")
axR.annotate("", xy=(0, 0.62), xytext=(0, 0),
             arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=ACCENT, lw=2.5))
axR.text(0.2, 0.6, "point mass 0.7\nat $x=0$", fontsize=8.5, color=ACCENT)
axR.set_xlabel("payout $x$"); axR.set_ylabel("density / mass")
axR.set_title("No ordinary density exists"); axR.set_xlim(-0.6, 12)
axR.legend(fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Heavy tails: the density exists, but its summaries may not {#sec-heavy-tails}

A subtler edge: a perfectly valid density can have a tail so heavy that quantities we will
soon want — like the mean $\int x f(x)\,dx$ — *do not exist* (the integral diverges). The
**Cauchy** density $f(x)=\tfrac{1}{\pi(1+x^2)}$ is the standard example: it integrates to
$1$ (a legitimate density), but $\int |x| f(x)\,dx$ diverges, so it has no mean. Nothing in
*this* article breaks — every interval still has a well-defined probability via
@thm-interval — but it is an honest flag that "has a density" does not guarantee "has a
mean." That boundary is exactly where [PROB-05 *Expectation*] picks up, and we hand it off
there.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is the continuous-density model the right tool, and when is it the wrong one?"* The
summary you should be able to recite:

- **Use a density** when the quantity is genuinely a measurement on a continuum (time,
  length, mass, concentration, error), recorded at a resolution *fine* relative to its
  spread, with no value carrying special mass.
- **Do not use it** when the variable is intrinsically discrete (counts, categories — use a
  pmf, [PROB-03]); when a single value carries real probability (use a mixed/two-part model,
  @sec-mixed); or when the recording grid is coarse relative to the spread (the histogram
  is a few spikes — treat it as discrete).
- **And remember the rate principle:** $f$ is probability *per unit width*, so it can
  exceed $1$, only its integral over a set is meaningful, and any change of variable must
  carry its Jacobian. The number you can interpret as a probability is always an
  *integral* of $f$, never a value of $f$.
:::

# How you would actually compute with densities {#sec-implementation}

The math is primary; this is the secondary, practical note. You almost never integrate a
named density by hand in practice. Every numerical stack ships the CDF and its inverse:
`scipy.stats.expon.cdf(2, scale=2)` returns $P(X\le2)$ for the rate-$0.5$ exponential
($0.632$ above), `expon.pdf` returns the density value $f(x)$, `expon.ppf(0.9)` inverts the
CDF to find the $90$th-percentile wait, and `expon.rvs` draws samples. Probabilities of
intervals are always computed as **CDF differences** $F(b)-F(a)$ — never by re-integrating —
because the CDF is the numerically stable, tabulated object (this is why the normal's CDF
$\Phi$ is the function statistics tables were built around). For a *custom* density with no
closed-form CDF, you integrate numerically (`scipy.integrate.quad`) and, if you need samples,
use inverse-transform sampling (feed uniforms through `ppf`) — which is itself the
change-of-variables theorem of @sec-change-of-vars run in reverse, and the seed of the
probability integral transform in [PROB-23]. You now know what every one of these calls is
doing and why the CDF, not the density, is the object they hang everything on.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[PROB-02 *Random Variables*]**, which built the CDF as the universal
descriptor of any random number and split the world into discrete and continuous. Its
sibling **[PROB-03 *Discrete RVs: the pmf*]** handled the countable case with probability
*mass*; this article handled the continuum with probability *density*, the two meeting at
the same CDF. From here the trunk continues:

- **[PROB-05 *Expectation: The Long-Run Average*]** — now that we can integrate against a
  density, the mean of a continuous variable is $E[X]=\int x f(x)\,dx$, the direct analogue
  of the discrete weighted sum. The Cauchy edge case of @sec-heavy-tails is exactly the
  divergence it must confront.
- **[PROB-13 *The Normal Distribution*]** and **[PROB-14 *Gamma, Exponential & Beta*]** —
  the named continuous distributions, each a specific density whose normalization
  (@thm-valid-density) and properties we will derive; the exponential of @sec-exponential
  gets its full memoryless/Poisson-process story in [PROB-14].
- **[PROB-18 *Linear Transforms of Random Vectors*]** — the change-of-variables rule of
  @sec-change-of-vars, lifted from one variable to many, where the scalar stretch factor
  $\bigl|\frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}\bigr|$ becomes the *determinant* of a Jacobian matrix.
- **[PROB-23 *Order Statistics & the Probability Integral Transform*]** — the inverse-CDF
  sampling sketched in @sec-implementation becomes the theorem that $F(X)\sim
  \mathrm{Uniform}(0,1)$, the reason a correctly computed $p$-value is uniform under the
  null.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The historical roots.** The leap from probability-on-points to probability-as-area runs
  through the geometric probability of Buffon's needle (1777) and was given its rigorous
  modern footing by Kolmogorov's 1933 *Grundbegriffe der Wahrscheinlichkeitsrechnung*, where
  the density appears as a Radon–Nikodym derivative of the probability measure with respect
  to length — the measure-theoretic form of "$f=F'$" that our absolutely-continuous
  qualifier in @def-continuous-rv was quietly pointing at.
- **The subtlety we flagged.** That there exist continuous CDFs with *no* density (the
  Cantor distribution) is the honest reason "continuous" and "absolutely continuous" are
  different words; the Lebesgue decomposition theorem (every distribution = discrete +
  absolutely continuous + singular) is the full statement, built in any measure-theoretic
  probability course.
- **Open threads this article deferred.** *What single number summarizes where a density
  sits, and when does it fail to exist?* — expectation and the Cauchy counterexample,
  [PROB-05]. *How do densities of two variables combine?* — joint densities and the
  double-integral version of @thm-interval, [PROB-07]. *Why is the bell curve's
  normalizing constant $1/\sqrt{2\pi}$?* — the Gaussian integral, the one place this track
  spends a genuine double integral, [PROB-13].
